data.title: Is Rockwool (CPSE:ROCK B) Share Price Slide Creating A Potential Opportunity For Investors
data.description:

  • The stock has been choppy, with a 4.7% decline over the last 7 days, a 6.3% gain over 30 days, and longer-term returns of a 17.2% decline year to date and a 35.4% decline over 1 year, while the 3-year return sits at 19.9% and the 5-year return at a 24.0% decline.
  • Recent coverage around Rockwool has focused on how the share price path and current valuation relate to broader investor sentiment and sector conditions. This context matters because it shapes how the market is weighing the balance between potential resilience and perceived risks for the stock.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Rockwool currently scores 3 out of 6. This suggests the stock screens as undervalued on half of the tested metrics. Next up is a closer look at the usual valuation tools such as DCF, multiples and comparables, followed by a different way of thinking about valuation that can give you a clearer overall picture.

Find out why Rockwool's -35.4% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: Rockwool Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes estimates of future cash flows, then discounts them back to today to arrive at an estimated intrinsic value per share. It is essentially asking what those future euros are worth in today's money.

For Rockwool, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is €204.95 million. Analyst estimates and subsequent extrapolations point to projected free cash flow of €270.00 million in 2030, with intermediate annual forecasts between 2026 and 2035 supplied by a mix of analyst inputs and growth estimates.

Discounting these projected cash flows back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of DKK 218.88 per share, compared with the recent share price of DKK 185.80. On this model, the shares screen as about 15.1% below the DCF estimate, which indicates that Rockwool currently appears undervalued on a cash flow basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Rockwool Price vs Sales

For profitable and revenue generating companies, the P/S ratio can be a useful yardstick because it anchors the share price to the actual revenue base, which tends to be more stable than earnings in cyclical periods.

What counts as a “normal” P/S ratio usually reflects how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk profile. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk often supports a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk tends to justify a lower one.

Rockwool currently trades on a P/S ratio of 1.33x. This sits above the Building industry average of 1.01x and also above the peer group average of 1.18x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Rockwool is 2.43x, which is its proprietary estimate of an appropriate P/S multiple given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk characteristics.

The Fair Ratio aims to be a more tailored guide than a simple peer or industry comparison. It adjusts for the company’s specific profile rather than assuming all Building stocks deserve the same multiple. With Rockwool’s actual P/S of 1.33x below the Fair Ratio of 2.43x, the shares currently screen as undervalued on this metric.

data.title: Thermo Fisher Refocuses On Biopharma With Microbiology Sale And New Center
data.description:
• Thermo Fisher Scientific (NYSE:TMO) agreed to sell its microbiology business to Astorg for more than US$1b.
  • The transaction reflects a shift in focus away from specialty diagnostics toward biopharma services and biologics.
  • The company also opened a new U.S. Bioprocess Design Center aimed at supporting biologics development and manufacturing.

For you as an investor, these moves highlight where Thermo Fisher Scientific, a major player in life science tools and diagnostics, is choosing to allocate capital and attention. The microbiology divestiture and the new U.S. Bioprocess Design Center point to a heavier emphasis on supporting biopharma customers and advanced therapies such as biologics.

This matters if you are tracking how NYSE:TMO aligns its portfolio with areas that management views as higher priority over the long run. The combination of a more focused business mix and added bioprocess capabilities could affect how the company generates revenue across tools, services, and solutions for pharmaceutical and biotech clients.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Thermo Fisher Scientific by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Thermo Fisher Scientific.

5 things going right for Thermo Fisher Scientific that this headline doesn't cover.

The microbiology sale to Astorg for about US$1.1b and the new U.S. Bioprocess Design Center both point to a tighter focus on biopharma services and biologics, rather than lower growth, more fragmented diagnostics niches. For you, that means a bit less exposure to traditional lab testing and a deeper tilt toward higher complexity work for pharmaceutical and biotech customers, where players like Danaher, Sartorius and Merck KGaA also compete. Management has already been active on capital allocation, with Q1 2026 sales of US$11.0b, net income of US$1.65b and a US$3.0b buyback of 4.9m shares, so an extra US$1.1b of proceeds increases flexibility to fund capacity, M&A or further returns to shareholders.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Thermo Fisher Scientific Narrative

  • The divestiture and bioprocess build out align with the narrative that Thermo Fisher is leaning into end to end pharma and biotech partnerships, from development to manufacturing and clinical research.
  • Shifting away from specialty diagnostics could challenge parts of the story that lean on broad diversification, since a smaller diagnostics footprint may reduce balance across end markets.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on clinical research, analytical tools and manufacturing platforms, and may not yet fully reflect how a leaner portfolio and the new Bioprocess Design Center change the mix of recurring service and consumables revenue.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Thermo Fisher Scientific to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, so using deal proceeds and new investments carefully is important if Thermo Fisher wants to keep balance sheet risk contained.
  • ⚠️ Selling a diagnostics unit that serves clinical and food safety markets increases reliance on biopharma spending, which can be sensitive to funding cycles and competition from peers such as Danaher and Agilent.
  • 🎁 The US$1.1b divestiture, together with the completed US$3.0b buyback and Q1 earnings growth, gives management multiple levers to refine the portfolio and capital structure in line with its long term priorities.
  • 🎁 The U.S. Bioprocess Design Center adds technical depth around biologics development and manufacturing, which could help Thermo Fisher win more process development and outsourcing work from pharma and biotech clients.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how management explains the earnings impact of the microbiology sale, including the projected US$0.15 dilution to adjusted EPS in the first full year after closing, and what it plans to do with the cash. Pay attention to disclosures on utilization and customer uptake at the Bioprocess Design Center, since that will indicate how compelling clients find the expanded biologics offering. It also helps to track segment level performance across specialty diagnostics, pharma services and analytical instruments to see whether the portfolio tilt is making earnings more dependent on biopharma cycles or smoothing them out.

data.title: Is It Worth Considering Caixa Seguridade Participações S.A. (BVMF:CXSE3) For Its Upcoming Dividend?
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see Caixa Seguridade Participações S.A. (BVMF:CXSE3) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next four days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is two business days before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is important as the process of settlement involves at least two full business days. So if you miss that date, you would not show up on the company's books on the record date. This means that investors who purchase Caixa Seguridade Participações' shares on or after the 4th of May will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 15th of May.

The company's next dividend payment will be R$0.33 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of R$1.31 to shareholders. Based on the last year's worth of payments, Caixa Seguridade Participações has a trailing yield of 7.2% on the current stock price of R$18.16. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. We need to see whether the dividend is covered by earnings and if it's growing.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Last year Caixa Seguridade Participações paid out 92% of its profits as dividends to shareholders, suggesting the dividend is not well covered by earnings.

Generally, the higher a company's payout ratio, the more the dividend is at risk of being reduced.

Fortunately for readers, Caixa Seguridade Participações's earnings per share have been growing at 19% a year for the past five years.

Caixa Seguridade Participações has delivered 28% dividend growth per year on average over the past four years. It's exciting to see that both earnings and dividends per share have grown rapidly over the past few years.

That combination signals the market is assigning a premium to OBICLtd’s earnings relative to the wider industry, even if it trades at a discount to closer peers. data.title: Forvia Refocuses On Technology As Interiors Sale And New Chair Reshape Path
data.description:
• Forvia (ENXTPA:FRVIA) has agreed to sell its Interiors business group to Apollo, reshaping its portfolio focus.
  • The company also announced the resignation of long serving Chairman and Board Member Michel de Rosen.
  • Pierre-André de Chalendar is proposed as the new Chairman, signaling a fresh phase for Forvia's board leadership.

Forvia, a major automotive supplier, has been repositioning around technology focused areas such as electronics, clean mobility and advanced seat structures. The exit from Interiors fits into this shift, moving capital and management attention away from lower margin, more commoditized activities toward higher tech content within the vehicle.

For investors, the combination of a portfolio reshuffle and a change in the chair role means both the business mix and governance framework are evolving at the same time. It is worth watching how the Apollo transaction terms, use of proceeds and the new board leadership influence Forvia's balance sheet, investment priorities and approach to partnerships over the coming years.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Forvia by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Forvia.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 3 things going right for Forvia that every investor should see.

The Interiors sale to Apollo, valued at €1.82b, sits at the heart of Forvia’s IGNITE plan to concentrate on technology driven areas such as electronics and clean mobility. You are seeing a classic portfolio reshaping move, with a sizeable, more commoditized activity carved out so that capital and management time can be directed toward higher content systems that often sit closer to the “brain” of the vehicle. The timing, shortly after Q1 2026 group sales of €5,135 million against €5,488 million a year earlier, underlines how important capital allocation and balance sheet strength have become for Forvia as it works through restructuring and past losses.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Forvia Narrative

  • The transaction is consistent with the existing narrative that Forvia is using divestments and portfolio streamlining to focus on higher value technology and improve earnings quality and cash generation.
  • Relying on sizable asset disposals like the Interiors sale also ties back to concerns in the narrative about execution risk around disposals and the impact on leverage if deals are delayed or terms change.
  • The combination of a large carve out and a potential new Chairman in Pierre-André de Chalendar may bring governance and oversight dimensions that are not fully reflected in the earlier narrative.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Forvia to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk around closing the €1.82b Interiors sale, including regulatory approvals, employee consultations and carve out complexity, could affect timing and balance sheet plans.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged that interest payments are not well covered by earnings, so if disposal proceeds or cost savings are slower than expected, financial flexibility could stay tight.
  • 🎁 The sale supports the company’s goal of concentrating on higher value added electronics and clean mobility, which could improve business mix versus peers such as Continental, Valeo and Magna.
  • 🎁 Portfolio simplification and potential deleveraging from the transaction may reduce earnings volatility over time and make future restructuring programs more manageable.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on three things: first, how close the final Interiors deal terms are to the €1.82b enterprise value and how proceeds are used, for example to cut debt or reinvest in electronics and clean mobility. Second, the pace at which Forvia’s revenue mix shifts following the sale and whether new orders in electronics and advanced seating offset the loss of Interiors sales. Third, how the board transition plays out after the June 4, 2026 AGM and whether the new chair sets clear targets around leverage, returns and capital deployment. These factors will shape how Forvia stacks up against large auto suppliers such as Continental and Magna in the next phase.

Why Tokai Rika’s delayed earnings matter for shareholders

Tokai Rika (TSE:6995) recently postponed its fiscal 2026 earnings release after identifying an error in the accounting treatment of tax effects tied to defined benefit obligations, prompting a multi year revision of past financial statements.

The postponed earnings have landed at a time when the share price, now at ¥2,887, has seen weaker short term momentum, with a 1 month share price return showing a 4.56% decline, even though the 5 year total shareholder return of 97.75% highlights a much stronger longer term result.

With Tokai Rika’s share price under pressure in the short term but supported by strong multi year returns and an accounting clean up in progress, you have to ask: is there still a buying opportunity here, or is future growth already priced in?

Preferred P/E of 7.8x: Is it justified?

On simple earnings terms, Tokai Rika trades on a P/E of 7.8x, which sits below both the wider JP market and the JP Auto Components industry, while the last close sits at ¥2,887.

The P/E multiple compares the current share price to earnings per share and is often used for businesses like Tokai Rika that already generate profit. A lower P/E can suggest the market is not paying a high price for current earnings, or that it is cautious about how sustainable those earnings will be.

Here, the picture is mixed. Earnings growth over the past year of 54%, and 23.3% per year over five years, is strong, and margins currently sit at 5%, higher than the 3.4% level reported previously. Yet analysts forecast an average earnings decline of 1.6% per year over the next three years, and expect revenue growth of 2.1% per year, which is slower than both the 6% forecast for the JP market and the 20% threshold often used to flag high growth. That kind of outlook can help explain why the market is not assigning a richer earnings multiple.

Compared with peers, the valuation gap is clear. The JP Auto Components industry trades at an average P/E of 9.8x, and the broader peer group sits at 54.3x. Tokai Rika screens as good value relative to both and is also close to its estimated fair P/E of 7.9x. That suggests the current 7.8x multiple is not an outlier and could be a level the market continues to reference when reassessing the shares.
